results.question: risc0-zk
results.verdict: ZK: QUALIFIED-NO (formally) / QUALIFIED-YES (mechanistically, succinct receipts only). RISC Zero's succinct STARK receipts DO include zero-knowledge blinding (recursion circuit appends ZK_CYCLES=1024 rows of random noise to data and accum columns, verified in code at tag v3.0.5 and main 2026-05-18), and RISC Zero states succinct/recursion proofs leak no execution-length information. However RISC Zero itself states, in the v3.0 versioned security-model doc and the live doc, that it only "targets perfect zero-knowledge" and "we have not written a mathematical argument to prove that our system is zero-knowledge," urging "those with critical privacy requirements to take caution." So there is NO formal ZK guarantee, but it is false that RISC Zero's only ZK path is a pairing-based wrap: the blinded succinct STARK receipt is the intended ZK artifact and is hash-based. Additionally (original code-level finding, no third-party source found): the v2 rv32im SEGMENT circuit used in risc0 2.x/3.x contains no blinding noise anywhere in its witness generation or prover (all randomness is Fiat-Shamir verifier randomness), so composite (per-segment) receipts in v2.x/3.x appear to be unblinded — only the recursion layer blinds. In v1.x the segment circuit DID blind and v1.1.0 strengthened it using Haböck–Kindi ePrint 2024/1037, implying pre-1.1.0 blinding was deficient. PQ: QUALIFIED-YES for the STARK receipts: RISC Zero's official table marks RISC-V Prover (96 bits) and Recursion Prover (99 bits) as "Quantum Safe? Yes" under Random Oracle Model + Toy Problem Conjecture, and the docs state STARK provers "are not known to be vulnerable to attacks via quantum computers" — i.e., conjectured/heuristic PQ, hash-based FRI, not a proven post-quantum reduction. The Groth16 (BN254) STARK-to-SNARK wrap is explicitly NOT quantum safe per the same table. IMPACT ON THESIS CLAIM: the claim collapses in its strong form for the RISC Zero substrate — a plausibly-PQ, blinded, ZK-targeting succinct receipt exists without any pairing-based wrap and is part of the standard affordable proving path (ReceiptKind::Succinct). The claim survives only in the weakened form: "no zkVM substrate offers a *formally proven* zero-knowledge guarantee on the hash-based path; RISC Zero ships ZK blinding but explicitly disclaims a ZK proof and cautions privacy-critical users; SP1 ships no ZK at the STARK layer at all." Version applicability: security-model ZK text is identical across versioned docs 1.0, 1.1, 1.2, 2.0.0, 2.1, 2.2, 2.3, 3.0 and the live page (fetched 2026-06-10); code findings verified at tag v3.0.5 (released 2026-02-03, latest stable) and main (commit 10fa978, 2026-05-18).

claims.claim: RISC Zero officially claims only to TARGET zero-knowledge and explicitly admits no formal ZK proof exists, urging caution for privacy-critical use. This text is identical in the version-3.0 docs (website/api_versioned_docs/version-3.0/security-model.md in the risc0 repo) and the live page.
claims.quote: The RISC Zero zkVM hides all program inputs and all witness data, and targets perfect zero-knowledge. Nevertheless, we have not written a mathematical argument to prove that our system is zero-knowledge. We are working on some minor engineering changes in order to make it easier to prove that our proof system has perfect zero-knowledge. We urge those with critical privacy requirements to take caution until the necessary changes have been implemented.
claims.bearing: Weakens the thesis claim's strong reading (RISC Zero receipts are not PROVEN ZK) but also weakens the claim that no non-pairing ZK path exists: RISC Zero positions the STARK itself as the ZK artifact.
claims.verify_note: Verified directly against raw source text, not just a summarizer. (1) Live page https://dev.risczero.com/api/security-model: fetched the HTML and grepped the stripped text — it contains, verbatim, under the heading "Zero-Knowledge Proving": "The RISC Zero zkVM hides all program inputs and all witness data, and targets perfect zero-knowledge." / "Nevertheless, we have not written a mathematical arg

claims.claim: RISC Zero documents that composite (segment) proofs leak execution length but that recursion (succinct) proofs leak no execution-length information — i.e., the succinct receipt is the privacy-relevant artifact.
claims.quote: Proofs generated by the RISC-V Prover that haven't been passed through the Recursion Prover leak information about the length of execution. Passing proofs through the Recursion Prover resolves this warning: recursion proofs leak no information about execution length.
claims.bearing: Supports ZK-preservation through the recursion/succinct layer; against the thesis claim that the affordable zkVM proof is necessarily non-ZK on RISC Zero.
claims.verify_note: VERIFIED VERBATIM. Fetched https://dev.risczero.com/api/security-model directly (curl, raw HTML, 2026-06-10) and found the exact quoted sentence in the "Zero-Knowledge Proving" section, as a 'warning' callout: "Proofs generated by the RISC-V Prover that haven't been passed through the Recursion Prover leak information about the length of execution. Passing proofs through the Recursion Prover resol

claims.claim: RISC Zero's official security table marks both STARK provers as quantum safe and the Groth16 wrap as not quantum safe. Table (v3.0 versioned doc, verbatim rows): 'RISC-V Prover | - Random Oracle Model / - Toy Problem Conjecture | 96 | Yes', 'Recursion Prover | - Random Oracle Model / - Toy Problem Conjecture | 99 | Yes', 'STARK-to-SNARK Prover | - Security of elliptic curve pairing over BN254. / - Knowledge of Exponent assumption / - Integrity of Groth16 Trusted Setup Ceremony | 99+ | No'.
claims.quote: The RISC-V Prover and the Recursion Prover both use STARK-based protocols, which are not known to be vulnerable to attacks via quantum computers. [...] The security of this part of the protocol depends on elliptic curve cryptography, and is therefore vulnerable to attacks from quantum computers.
claims.bearing: Supports PQ=qualified-yes for the hash-based STARK receipts (conjectured, 'not known to be vulnerable', ROM + Toy Problem Conjecture — not a PQ security proof); confirms the Groth16 wrap is non-PQ as the thesis says.
claims.verify_note:
Verified directly against the live page at https://dev.risczero.com/api/security-model (fetched 2026-06-10), cross-checked two ways: WebFetch and an independent raw-HTML curl with verbatim text extraction (so the result does not depend on a summarizer model echoing the claim back).

1. Version match: the page header reads verbatim "Cryptographic Security Model Version: 3.0" — matches the claim's "

claims.claim: RISC Zero's protocol docs describe explicit ZK blinding: random noise appended to trace columns. (Same statement appears in 'STARK by Hand' Lesson 3: 'Before encoding each column as a polynomial, we append random padding to the end of the Execution Trace, which adds the entropy needed to hide the user's data, allowing for a zero-knowledge protocol.')
claims.quote: The Prover adds some random `noise` to the end of those columns in order to ensure that the protocol is zero-knowledge.
claims.bearing: Against the thesis claim: RISC Zero's STARK protocol includes designed ZK blinding, unlike SP1's default STARKs. Caveat: these docs describe the original (v1-era) protocol; see the code-level finding below for v2/v3 segment circuits.
claims.verify_note:
VERIFIED against the live source (fetched 2026-06-10, raw HTML, not just the summarizing fetch model).

1. Cited source (https://dev.risczero.com/proof-system/proof-system-sequence-diagram, HTTP 200, no redirect): the page contains, verbatim (after tag-stripping): "After computing the data columns and auxiliary/accum columns, the Prover adds some random noise to the end of those columns in order t

results.question: prior-art-s2morrow-policharla
results.verdict: No single threat kills the thesis novelty claim ("first same-hardware same-scheme measurement of PQ signature verification cost in a general-purpose zkVM vs a static circuit-SNARK, with a custom large-prime-field Griffin precompile, applied to BDEC anonymous credentials over PLUM/Loquat"). Two threats NARROW it materially; three are orthogonal. (1) s2morrow [NARROWS, and the orchestrator's belief was factually wrong]: it is NOT Dilithium/ML-DSA — it is Falcon512 + SPHINCS+-128s batch verification inside the Cairo zkVM proven with Stwo (StarkWare-bitcoin org, aggregation-for-Bitcoin context). It occupies "PQ signature verification inside a zkVM," so the thesis cannot claim that bare territory; but it has NO published proving benchmarks (roadmap item still unchecked), no zkVM-vs-circuit-SNARK comparison, no large-prime-field precompile (Falcon/SPHINCS+ have no 192-bit field-mismatch problem over M31), and no anonymous-credential application. (2) Policharla et al. ePrint 2023/414 [NARROWS — closest on the application axis]: PQ anonymous credentials / Privacy Pass by proving zkDilithium (a STARK-friendly Dilithium2 variant) signature verification in a hand-written Winterfell STARK over a custom field; also makes the "flexibility of general-purpose ZK" argument the thesis uses. It pre-empts "PQ anoncreds via general-purpose ZK proofs of signature verification" (2023) — the thesis must scope its novelty to the substrate comparison + precompile + unmodified-scheme measurement, NOT to the generic PQ-anoncred construction. It uses no zkVM and modifies the signature scheme rather than measuring the cost of an unmodified one. (3) zk-creds (Rosenberg et al., IEEE S&P 2023) [ORTHOGONAL]: pairing-based zkSNARK anoncreds from existing identity documents (passports); not post-quantum, no zkVM, no substrate cost comparison — it is BDEC's lineage, not the thesis's claim. (4) Arguzz (arXiv 2509.10819) [ORTHOGONAL, citable as motivation]: a metamorphic-testing + fault-injection FUZZER for zkVM soundness/completeness bugs (not formal verification of AIRs); found 11 bugs across RISC Zero/Nexus/Jolt+3 others incl. a $50k RISC Zero bounty — strengthens, rather than threatens, the thesis's case for written soundness arguments on custom precompile AIRs. (5) Pegasus/PegaRing (ePrint 2025/1841, Oct 2025, Monash group overlapping Loquat's authors) [ORTHOGONAL to the measurement claim, but NARROWS the PLUM/Loquat motivation]: a new Σ-protocol signature from the same Legendre/power-residue PRF family with 6–8 KB signatures and ms-level sign/verify (far smaller/faster standalone than Loquat), plus a QROM-secure ring signature giving native anonymity. It does not claim SNARK-friendly verification and measures nothing in a zkVM/SNARK, so the thesis's niche (SNARK-friendly verification cost inside proof systems) survives — but it must be cited as concurrent work, and the "why PLUM/Loquat at all" motivation must rest explicitly on SNARK-friendly verification, since for standalone signing Pegasus now dominates. NET ACTION: novelty prose must (a) drop any implicit "first PQ sig verification in a zkVM" or "first PQ anoncreds via general ZK" reading, (b) anchor on the comparative same-hardware measurement + large-prime-field algebraic-hash precompile + unmodified scheme, (c) cite s2morrow, 2023/414, and Pegasus as adjacent/concurrent work.

claims.claim: THREAT 1 — s2morrow. Citation: starkware-bitcoin/s2morrow, GitHub repository (no accompanying paper found), 'S2morrow: STARK-based aggregation of quantum-resistant signatures', 2025. What it does: batch verification of post-quantum signatures (Falcon512, SPHINCS+-128s with SHA2/Blake2s) implemented in the Cairo zkVM and proven with StarkWare's Stwo Circle-STARK prover, motivated by Bitcoin signature aggregation (BIP360); goal is comparing proving time across schemes and benchmarking proof size/verification time vs LaBRADOR. The orchestrator's prior belief is WRONG on two counts: it is not Dilithium/ML-DSA, and it is signature schemes Falcon + SPHINCS+ on Cairo/Stwo. VERDICT: NARROWS. It occupies 'PQ signature verification inside a zkVM' (so that bare claim is not novel), but its 'Stwo proving benchmarks' roadmap item is still unchecked (no published measurements), there is no zkVM-vs-static-circuit-SNARK same-hardware comparison, no custom large-prime-field precompile (Falcon/SPHINCS+ lack the 192-bit field-mismatch problem), and no anonymous-credential application.
claims.quote: STARK-based aggregation of quantum-resistant signatures. This project explores zkVM approach for batch verification of multiple PQ signatures. The goal is compare proving time for different signature schemes, as well as benchmark vs other approaches (e.g. LaBRADOR) in terms of proof size (compression ratio) and verification time. Implementation details: ZKVM: Cairo; STARK prover: Stwo; Signature schemes: NIST finalist — Falcon (lattice based), alternative Sphincs+ (hash based). Roadmap: [x] Falcon512 verification; [x] Sphincs+ 128s with SHA2 (simple mode) verification; [ ] Stwo proving benchmarks
claims.verify_note:
VERIFIED against the live source. I fetched the raw README via the GitHub API (https://api.github.com/repos/starkware-bitcoin/s2morrow/readme, Accept: application/vnd.github.raw) because raw.githubusercontent.com/main 404s — the default branch is `master`.

1. QUOTE REPRODUCED, near-verbatim. The README reads: "STARK-based aggregation of quantum-resistant signatures. / This project explores zkVM a

claims.claim: THREAT 2 — Policharla et al. Citation: Guru-Vamsi Policharla, Bas Westerbaan, Armando Faz-Hernández, Christopher A. Wood, 'Post-Quantum Privacy Pass via Post-Quantum Anonymous Credentials', Cryptology ePrint Archive, Paper 2023/414 (preprint, 2023-03-22; never flagged as published at a venue on ePrint). What it does: instantiates the generic Fischlin-style PQ anonymous credential construction by proving knowledge of a signature under zkDilithium — their own STARK-friendly modification of Dilithium2 (NTT-domain hashing with Poseidon) — inside a hand-written STARK circuit (Winterfell backend with custom fields, per the companion repo github.com/guruvamsi-policharla/zkdilithium: 'This project uses the winterfell crate as the backend for the STARK prover... a fork... which contains the zkDilithium fields/extensions'). Tokens 85–175 KB, generation 0.3–5 s, verification 20–30 ms, 115-bit proof security. Also explicitly claims the flexibility-of-general-purpose-ZK advantage. VERDICT: NARROWS — this is the closest prior art on the application axis. It pre-empts 'PQ anonymous credentials via general-purpose ZK proofs of PQ signature verification' (2023) and partially pre-empts the flexibility framing. It does NOT use a zkVM, does NOT compare proving substrates on the same hardware, and MODIFIES the signature scheme to fit the prover rather than measuring an unmodified scheme's substrate cost — exactly the gap the thesis's measurement claim should be scoped to.
claims.quote: It is known that one can generically construct a post-quantum anonymous credential scheme, supporting the showing of arbitrary predicates on its attributes using general-purpose zero-knowledge proofs secure against quantum adversaries [Fischlin, CRYPTO 2006]... To wit, a post-quantum Privacy Pass constructed in this way using zkDilithium, our proposal for a STARK-friendly variation on Dilithium2, allows for a trade-off between token size (85–175KB) and generation time (0.3–5s) with a proof security level of 115 bits... Finally, we point out a clear advantage of our approach: the flexibility afforded by the general purpose zero-knowledge proofs.
claims.verify_note:
VERIFIED against the primary sources (fetched eprint.iacr.org/2023/414 abstract page, the full PDF, and the companion repo README). Findings:

1. QUOTE: Reproduced essentially verbatim from the ePrint abstract. Found exactly: "It is known that one can generically construct a post-quantum anonymous credential scheme, supporting the showing of arbitrary predicates on its attributes using general-pur

claims.claim: THREAT 3 — zk-creds. Citation: Michael Rosenberg, Jacob White, Christina Garman, Ian Miers, 'zk-creds: Flexible Anonymous Credentials from zkSNARKs and Existing Identity Infrastructure', 2023 IEEE Symposium on Security and Privacy, DOI 10.1109/SP46215.2023.00108; ePrint 2022/878. What it does: an anonymous-credential protocol using general-purpose zkSNARKs (pairing-based, Groth16-family arkworks stack — NOT post-quantum) to (1) issue credentials to a transparency log/blockchain without issuer signing keys, (2) convert existing identity documents (e.g., ICAO passports with classical signatures) into anonymous credentials without issuer cooperation, (3) compose flexible identity statements; ~150 ms showings. VERDICT: ORTHOGONAL to the thesis novelty claim. No post-quantum component, no zkVM, no proving-substrate cost comparison, no algebraic-hash precompile. It is prior art for BDEC's general lineage (anoncreds-from-existing-signatures-via-general-ZK), which the thesis inherits from BDEC rather than claims; cite it as background, not as a threat.
claims.quote: We present and build zk-creds, a protocol that uses general-purpose zero-knowledge proofs to 1) remove the need for credential issuers to hold signing keys: credentials can be issued to a bulletin board instantiated as a transparency log, Byzantine system, or even a blockchain; 2) convert existing identity documents into anonymous credentials without modifying documents or coordinating with their issuing authority; 3) allow for flexible, composable, and complex identity statements over multiple credentials. Concretely, identity assertions using zk-creds take less than 150ms in a real-world scenario of using a passport to anonymously access age-restricted videos.
claims.verify_note: Verified against the live ePrint page and the full PDF (https://eprint.iacr.org/2022/878, downloaded with browser UA after a 403 on default fetch). (1) The quoted abstract sentence appears verbatim, including all three numbered points and the "less than 150ms" passport scenario. (2) Citation metadata matches exactly: Rosenberg/White/Garman/Miers, 2023 IEEE S&P, DOI 10.1109/SP46215.2023.00108, ePri

claims.claim: THREAT 4 — Arguzz. Citation: 'Arguzz: Testing zkVMs for Soundness and Completeness Bugs', arXiv:2509.10819 (September 2025; authors per Semantic Scholar listing include Hochrainer and Wüstholz; also presented at Devconnect ARG). What it does: the first automated FUZZING tool (metamorphic testing of semantically-equivalent Rust program pairs + fault injection into the VM to mimic malicious/buggy provers) for finding soundness and completeness bugs in zkVMs; tested six real-world zkVMs (RISC Zero, Nexus, Jolt, SP1, OpenVM, Pico) and found eleven bugs in three of them, including a RISC Zero bug that earned a $50,000 bounty despite prior audits. It is NOT formal verification of AIR constraints — the orchestrator's belief ('formal verification of AIR constraints') overstates it. VERDICT: ORTHOGONAL to the novelty claim — it measures nothing about PQ signatures, precompiles, or substrate cost. Bearing on the thesis is SUPPORTIVE: documented constraint-level soundness bugs in RISC Zero and SP1 (the thesis's two platforms) are direct motivation for the thesis's per-precompile written soundness arguments, and a caveat for the assumption that the zkVM's lookup argument is sound.
claims.quote: Arguzz is the first automated tool for testing zkVMs for soundness and completeness bugs, combining a novel variant of metamorphic testing with fault injection... Arguzz was used to test six real-world zkVMs (RISC Zero, Nexus, Jolt, SP1, OpenVM, and Pico) and found eleven bugs in three of them. Notably, one RISC Zero bug resulted in a $50,000 bounty, despite prior audits. [paraphrase of abstract as rendered by arXiv fetch; fuzzing tool, not formal AIR verification]
claims.verify_note: Fetched https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.10819 directly. The source confirms every load-bearing element of the claim. Title verbatim: "Arguzz: Testing zkVMs for Soundness and Completeness Bugs". Authors: Christoph Hochrainer, Valentin Wüstholz, Maria Christakis (matches "authors per Semantic Scholar listing include Hochrainer and Wüstholz"). Submitted September 13, 2025 (matches "September 2025"). Abstr

results.question: prior-art-fresh-sweep
results.verdict: NO KILL FOUND. The full conjunction claim — measured proving-cost comparison of PLUM/Loquat-family (Legendre/power-residue-PRF, algebraic hash over a large prime field) signature verification between a general-purpose zkVM with a custom Griffin-permutation precompile and a static R1CS SNARK (Aurora), on consumer hardware, framed by PQ anonymous credentials — survives the sweep. However, three flanks are NARROWED and the word "first" must be scoped tightly. (1) Closest competitor: CAPSS (Feneuil & Rivain, eprint 2025/061, revised Oct 2025) now occupies "SNARK-friendly PQ signatures from AO permutations incl. Griffin + anonymous-credentials application + MEASURED Aurora/libiop proving numbers (with zk-enabled executables)" — it claims 5-8x fewer R1CS than Loquat, so PLUM/Loquat cannot be presented as the constraint-count SOTA, and 'first measured Aurora proving of an AO-hash PQ signature' is not claimable (Loquat's own paper already did that too). CAPSS does NOT touch zkVMs — the zkVM arm and the cross-substrate comparison remain unclaimed territory. (2) "PQ signature verification inside a zkVM" is no longer virgin: HAPPIER (LightSec 2025, Springer Jan 2026) proves XMSS aggregation in RISC Zero on a standard laptop (built-in SHA precompile, no custom precompile, no SNARK comparison arm); a Dec 2025 practitioner library verifies Dilithium in SP1 (5.6M cycles, 22s network proofs, guest-code NTT gadget, not a precompile); leanMultisig benchmarks XMSS aggregation on an M4 Max in a special-purpose hash-based zkVM. None covers algebraic-hash-over-large-prime-field schemes or authors a custom AIR precompile. (3) The thesis's "cycle reduction does not equal prove-time reduction" finding has independent corroboration (Anoma forum benchmark, Feb 2026: k256 precompile cuts cycles 17-39x with only modest proving gains) — cite as corroboration, do not claim sole observation. ANGLES RETURNING NOTHING: (a) no Falcon or SPHINCS+ verification measured inside SP1/RISC Zero/Jolt found (Falcon work is all EVM/Solidity/EIP-precompile); no PQ-signature custom precompile in any zkVM found; (b) no PQ anonymous-credential showing measured inside a general-purpose zkVM found; (c) no third-party PLUM implementation or benchmark found (only the PLUM paper itself); no Griffin or any large-prime-field AO-hash precompile exists in SP1 or RISC Zero (SP1 issue #2315 confirms absence and unmet demand as of mid-2025); (e) no academic study comparing proving cost of the SAME cryptographic predicate between a zkVM and a static R1CS SNARK found — only heterogeneous-workload benchmark suites (Fenbushi 8-zkVM testbed, babybear-labs repo incl. Circom, risc0/zkvm-perf). Caveat: ronin207/loquat-vc surfaced as a search hit but is the Operator's own repository (git remote confirms ronin207) — not external prior art. Defensive scoping recommendation: claim "first measured same-predicate proving-cost comparison of an algebraic-hash PQ signature (Loquat/PLUM family) between a general-purpose zkVM with a custom large-prime-field permutation precompile and a static R1CS SNARK on consumer hardware" and add CAPSS, HAPPIER, and the SP1-Dilithium library to related work.

claims.claim: CAPSS (Feneuil & Rivain, CryptoExperts; eprint 2025/061, last revised 2025-10-03, preprint) constructs SNARK-friendly PQ signatures from AO permutations including Griffin, claims 5-8x fewer R1CS constraints than Loquat, and — since the Oct 2025 revision — includes an anonymous-credentials application. This is the closest external competitor on the 'SNARK-friendly PQ signature + PQ anon creds' flank. It contains no zkVM measurement.
claims.quote: This represents a 4-6x reduction in signature size and a 5-8x reduction in R1CS constraints compared to Loquat (CRYPTO 2024), a SNARK-friendly post-quantum signature scheme based on the Legendre PRF. We showcase CAPSS through aggregated signatures, achieving sub-kilobyte amortized size for large batches, and anonymous credentials, enabling presentation proofs under 150 KB
claims.bearing: narrows — kills any 'Loquat/PLUM is the R1CS-count state of the art' framing and any 'first PQ anon-cred design from SNARK-friendly symmetric signatures' framing; leaves the zkVM arm and the cross-substrate comparison untouched
claims.verify_note: Verified directly against https://eprint.iacr.org/2025/061 (HTML metadata page) and the full PDF (2025/061.pdf, text-extracted). (1) The QUOTE appears verbatim in the abstract (PDF uses typographic en-dashes/multiplication signs: "4–6×", "5–8×"; content identical). (2) Authors confirmed: Thibauld Feneuil and Matthieu Rivain, both listed as "CryptoExperts (France)". (3) Status confirmed: "Preprint.

claims.claim: CAPSS ships a libiop/Aurora wrapper and used it to produce measured Aurora proving numbers (Table 6: 1 signature aggregated in 2.6 s, verification 0.5 s, ~128 KB proof at <=2^15 R1CS; up to 1024 sigs), with zk-enabled prover executables. So measured Aurora proving cost of an AO-hash PQ-signature verification predicate already exists externally (in addition to the Loquat paper's own 32-signature Aurora aggregation at ~7 min).
claims.quote: This wrapper has been used to produce the aggregation performance numbers in Table 6 of the CAPSS article: ... | t_aggr (s) | 2.6 | 12 | 49 | 208 | 906 | 3872 | ... Executables with `zk` in the file name produce *zero-knowledge* proofs.
claims.bearing: narrows — the Aurora-side (Cell 4 analog) measurement is not novel in isolation; the novelty must live in the zkVM-vs-Aurora same-predicate comparison and the precompile arm
claims.verify_note: VERIFIED against the live repo and the paper. The README at github.com/CryptoExperts/capss-aggregation contains the quote verbatim: "This wrapper has been used to produce the aggregation performance numbers in Table 6 of the CAPSS article:" followed by the exact table (t_aggr 2.6/12/49/208/906/3872 s for 1/4/16/64/256/1024 sigs; t_verif 0.5 s and |proof| 128 KB at #R1CS <=2^15 for n=1), and verbat

claims.claim: HAPPIER (Saygan, Gündoğan, Arslan, Gönen; LightSec 2025, LNCS 16216, Springer, published online 2026-01-31, DOI 10.1007/978-3-032-15541-2_1) implements PQ aggregate signatures (XMSS) efficiently with RISC Zero, running on a standard laptop, disabling Groth16 composition for PQ security. It is a peer-reviewed PQ-signature-inside-RISC-Zero measurement on consumer hardware — but uses hash-based XMSS (SHA precompile-friendly), authors no custom precompile, and has no circuit-SNARK comparison arm.
claims.quote: In contrast, our scheme generates slightly larger signatures (2–3 MB) but drastically reduces aggregation time and can handle up to 2^16 signatures on a standard laptop ... Although STARK is the primary proof system used in RISC0-zkVM, it incorporates proof composition with Groth16 to reduce proof sizes [29]. In our implementation, we disabled the Groth16 composition to maintain post-quantum security.
claims.bearing: narrows — 'first PQ signature verification measured inside a zkVM on consumer hardware' is not claimable unqualified; scope to algebraic-hash large-prime-field schemes with custom precompile and cross-substrate comparison
claims.verify_note: Fetched the Springer page (after following the idp.springer.com cookie redirect). The claim's bibliographic facts all match: "HAPPIER: Hash-Based, Aggregatable, Practical Post-quantum Signatures Implemented Efficiently with Risc0" by Saygan, Gündoğan, Arslan, Gönen; LightSec 2025; LNCS 16216 pp. 3-22; First Online 31 January 2026; DOI 10.1007/978-3-032-15541-2_1. Both quoted passages were reproduc

claims.claim: A practitioner library (Kota, Medium, 2025-12-08) implements full Dilithium (ML-DSA) signature verification in the SP1 zkVM via a guest-code NTT gadget: 5,625,411 total cycles, 22.07 s Groth16 proof via SP1 Network, 260-byte proof. Lattice scheme, no custom AIR precompile, network prover (not consumer hardware), no SNARK-circuit comparison.
claims.quote: I built a library for verifying NIST-standardized post-quantum Dilithium signatures using zero-knowledge proofs. 104 tests, 132K+ fuzzing iterations, and production-ready SP1 Network proofs (22 seconds, 260 bytes).
claims.bearing: narrows — PQ signature verification inside SP1 specifically already exists as grey literature; cite in related work and scope the thesis claim away from generic 'PQ sig in SP1'
claims.verify_note: QUOTE CONFIRMED VERBATIM. Fetched the Medium article (author Kota, 2025-12-08, title "Building a Zero-Knowledge Verifier for Dilithium Signatures: NTT Gadget Implementation in SP1 zkVM"). The TL;DR contains the exact quoted sentence word-for-word. All headline figures reproduce from the source: 5,625,411 total cycles (profiling table), "Proof type: Groth16 / Size: 260 bytes / Time: 22.07s", SP1 Ne

results.question: version-landscape
results.verdict: NO INVALIDATING DRIFT FOUND as of 2026-06-10. (a) SP1: latest release is v6.2.4 (2026-06-08); the only changes since the thesis pin v6.2.1 (2026-05-15) are v6.2.2 (gRPC channel reuse, hosted NetworkProver mode), v6.2.3 (ExecutionReport serde, Rust 1.94 toolchain), and v6.2.4 (two minor memory-hygiene fixes: decoupling gas-estimation chunk cadence from the memory chunk threshold, and releasing per-proof task bookkeeping/artifacts in SP1LocalNode). No shard-streaming rewrite, no documented peak-RSS reduction, and no Apple-silicon/Metal proving — SP1 GPU proving remains CUDA-only (Compute Capability >= 8.6, 24 GB VRAM, Docker + NVIDIA toolkit) and the hardware docs make zero mention of macOS/Metal; CPU proving guidance is 16GB+ and Groth16/PLONK aggregation guidance is 32GB+, consistent with the thesis's ~20 GB OOM on a 24 GB machine. SP1 Hypercube mainnet positioning (Feb 2026) targets 16x RTX 5090 clusters, orthogonal to laptop proving. (b) RISC Zero: stable line is still 3.0.x; latest is v3.0.5 (2026-02-03), whose backports include "Avoid unbounded host buffer allocation during execution" — the single drift item worth checking: if the thesis's 3.0.x pin predates 3.0.5, this host-memory fix could shift (likely lower) peak host RSS, though it is a bugfix backport, not an architecture change. v3.0.4 (2025-11-24) only exposed seal_to_json in risc0-groth16. A v5.0.0-rc.1 pre-release exists (2026-01-15) but its release body is essentially empty (a docker libtbb-dev build fix); no stable 4.x/5.x has shipped and no memory/recursion changes are documented for it. (c) ZK wrap: SP1's security-model docs STILL state individual STARK proofs do not satisfy zero-knowledge and Groth16/PLONK remain the only ZK path — no hash-based ZK wrap shipped or announced; searches for a 2026 non-pairing ZK mode announcement came up empty. RISC Zero, by contrast, has ALWAYS claimed its STARK receipts are zero-knowledge (docs.rs 3.0.5: publishing a receipt reveals no additional execution information), so the thesis should scope any "ZK requires a pairing-based wrap" claim to SP1; on RISC Zero the wall is different and unchanged: the Groth16 prover "only works on x86 architecture, and so Apple Silicon is currently unsupported" — an architectural wall, stronger than an OOM wall. RISC Zero does use Apple Metal compute cores by default for STARK proving, and its docs advise adjusting segment size when under 10 GB available memory (analogous to the thesis's SP1 shard-size 2^22 tuning). (d) No published prover-memory benchmarks on 16-32 GB consumer machines were found for 2026: the Vac/Logos report (Sept 2024) used a 64-core EPYC with 600 GiB RAM, zkbenchmarks.com uses 576 GB servers, and RISC Zero's official benchmarks page only tells users to generate their own datasheet — the thesis's 24 GB Apple-silicon measurements occupy an unpopulated niche. Caveat: all quotes below were extracted via a fetch-and-summarize pipeline; verbatim fidelity is as reported by the fetcher and exact wording should be spot-checked against the URLs before citing in the thesis.
